cards.back: A program currently loaded into memory and executing, tracked by the OS via a process control block (PCB).
A scheduling algorithm that gives each ready process a fixed time slice in turn, pre-empting it and moving it to the back of the queue if it isn't finished.
The fixed maximum amount of CPU time a process may run before the OS pre-empts it, in round-robin scheduling.
Saving the registers of the process being paused and loading the registers of the process being resumed, so execution can continue correctly later.
Dividing logical memory into fixed-size pages and physical memory into equal-size frames, so a process's pages can be scattered across any free frames.
Dividing a program into variable-size logical segments (e.g. code, data, stack) that reflect its structure.
Using disk storage as an extension of RAM, via a page table, so the total memory in use can exceed physical RAM.
An event triggered when a process accesses a page that isn't currently in RAM, causing the OS to load it in from disk.
A signal that causes the processor to pause its current instruction stream and transfer control to an interrupt service routine.
The operating system code that runs in response to a specific interrupt, e.g. reading a keystroke or handling a finished disk transfer.
Software that manages a computer's hardware and provides the platform other software runs on, e.g. the operating system and device drivers.
Systems software that performs one specific maintenance task, e.g. a disk defragmenter, antivirus scanner or backup tool.
Software written to let a user perform a real-world task unrelated to managing the computer itself, e.g. a word processor or web browser.
